title: 🎙️ Repair Culture & Practical Tech
description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Repair Culture & Practical Tech' — the right to repair movement, gear that outlasts its manufacturer, DIY fixes, and tools built for longevity.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder:
- 80-100: Directly about repairability, right-to-repair legislation wins or losses, teardowns scoring repairability (iFixit-style), gear designed for longevity or user serviceability, products outliving manufacturer support windows and how owners keep them running, permacomputing, vintage or legacy hardware still in active use, open-source hardware enabling long-term repairability, Louis Rossmann-style repair advocacy, 3D-printed replacement parts extending device life, battery replacement guides, rural or off-grid repair self-sufficiency.
- 55-79: Hands-on maker and DIY content (builds, modifications, hardware hacks, workshop projects), practical consumer electronics guides with real technical depth, 3D printing hardware and filament reviews, off-grid and solar power gear reviews, home lab hardware, tools and workshop equipment, battery and power tech — even without an explicit right-to-repair angle.
- 30-54: General gadget and device reviews or news with some hands-on or technical substance; consumer electronics that touch on software longevity or ecosystem lock-in; hardware products with open firmware or modular design as a selling point.
- 10-29: Surface-level product announcements, consumer gadget speculation without technical depth, leaked device specs, general tech news without a hardware or repair angle.
- 0-9: Geopolitical conflict, natural disaster (without engineering/preparedness angle), wildlife and conservation, working lands policy, Indigenous governance, science discoveries without DIY or hardware relevance, financial market news.

Strongly prefer: articles about the right-to-repair movement (legislation, court cases, company policies), repairability scores and teardowns, gear with documented longevity, open-source or user-serviceable hardware, stories of devices kept alive past manufacturer support end-of-life, and rural/off-grid self-sufficiency through repair. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Two Italian brothers claimed to have recorded dying Soviet cosmonauts that Moscow erased from history
items.content_html:
The Lost Cosmonauts theory alleges that the Soviet Union launched humans into space before Yuri Gagarin's 1961 flight — and that the cosmonauts onboard died. The strongest piece of supposed evidence came from Italian brothers Achille and Giovanni Battista Judica-Cordiglia, amateur radio operators in Turin who claimed to have intercepted transmissions from doomed Soviet spacecraft between 1960 and 1964. — Read the rest

The post Two Italian brothers claimed to have recorded dying Soviet cosmonauts that Moscow erased from history appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Japanese officers cannibalized four American POWs on a Pacific island in 1944
items.content_html:
In September 1944, nine American pilots were shot down during bombing raids on Chichijima, the largest island in the Bonin chain. Eight were captured. The ninth, who evaded capture, was 20-year-old George H. W. Bush.

The eight captured airmen — Lloyd Woellhof, Grady York, James Dye, Glenn Frazier Jr., — Read the rest

items.title: [Boing Boing] Ghislaine Maxwell reportedly finds her mean-girls table in prison
items.content_html:
Raw Story, citing the Daily Mail, says Maxwell has formed a "highly secretive" group at Federal Prison Camp Bryan in Texas, where she allegedly whispers with three women she considers among the "finest and best educated" prisoners. The reported crew has a certain country-club-crime theme: a doctor convicted in a bogus billing case, a former CFO accused of raiding company funds, and a bookkeeper who wrote herself very generous checks. — Read the rest

The post Ghislaine Maxwell reportedly finds her mean-girls table in prison appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The Fountain of Bacchino in Florence, Italy
items.content_html:
The Boboli Gardens in Florence are certainly not lacking in sculptures. There are no fewer than 288 pieces here, mostly created between the 16th and 18th centuries. The park is part of the Italian World Heritage Site ("Medici Villas and Gardens in Tuscany") and a major attraction for the roughly 4 million tourists who visit the city every year.

Most of these statues showcase ideal figures in line with their respective cultural eras: youths with theatrically outstretched arms, warriors in dramatic combat, women in seductive poses or showing maternal affection to a child. The bodies are slender, muscular, and defined—even those of older figures.

One sculpture, however, stands in diametric opposition to this classical fiction: located at the northeastern end and exit of the park is the Bacchus Fountain (Fontana di Bacchino). Sitting atop a large Moorish turtle that spews water from its mouth is a stark-naked, overweight little man, looking proudly to his right and holding out his right hand in a defensive gesture. His body is massive compared to the length of his limbs. Beneath his heavy belly sits the dwarf's "best piece," which forms the exact center of the sculpture and appears rather disproportionate. While the depiction of male genitalia was common in Renaissance plastic arts, this emphasis on his manhood is, without a doubt, a deliberately used comedic element. Thanks to this curious contrast to normality, the grotesque sculpture is world-famous and is sold as a miniature to tourists in Florence.

Depicted in this potentially degrading pose is a real-life historical figure: Nano Morgante, the famous court dwarf of the Medici from the reign of Cosimo I to Ferdinando I. He lived from roughly 1530 to 1584, and his real name was Braccio di Bartolo. He suffered from chondrodystrophy, a genetic form of dwarfism where abnormalities in cartilage and bone formation result in short arms and legs combined with a normal torso and a disproportionately large head. Even his nickname, "Dwarf Morgante," was ironic: Morgante was actually a giant from a popular epic poem of the time by Luigi Pulci.

However, di Bartolo must have been highly intelligent, as well as humorous, quick-witted, and diplomatic. He was highly esteemed as the favorite court jester and served as a close advisor to Cosimo I. The Grand Duke granted him personal freedoms, his own income, and even land ownership.

In 1560, Cosimo I de’ Medici commissioned the Florentine sculptor Valerio Cioli to create the work. Nano Morgante’s significance is evident in the fact that he repeatedly appears as a comedic or allegorical element in various paintings and sculptures of the Florentine Renaissance. Furthermore, the lightheartedness and humor of the Bacchus Fountain embody Cosimo I de’ Medici's life motto: Festina lente—Make haste slowly.
